questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a history of daily heavy alcohol use presents to the emergency department 6 hours after his last drink. He complains of tremor, sweating, anxiety, and mild nausea. Observations: pulse 100 bpm, BP 145/90 mmHg, temperature 37.1°C. He is alert, oriented, and has no hallucinations. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The early symptoms (tremor, sweating, anxiety) within 6-12 hours of last drink, with no hallucinations or confusion, are classic for uncomplicated alcohol withdrawal. Delirium tremens occurs later (48-96 hours) with confusion and hallucinations. Panic disorder, hyperthyroidism, and hypoglycaemia are differentials but the temporal relationship to alcohol cessation is key.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with known alcohol dependence presents 18 hours after his last drink. He has a coarse tremor, sweats, nausea, and insomnia, but no hallucinations. He is fully orientated. Bloods: normal glucose, normal thyroid function. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The absence of confusion, ataxia, or ophthalmoplegia makes Wernicke's encephalopathy unlikely. Delirium tremens requires hallucinations and agitation. Cerebellar degeneration is chronic. Panic disorder is not reliant on recent alcohol cessation. The typical timeline and symptoms point to uncomplicated alcohol withdrawal.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old homeless man with a long history of heavy alcohol intake is brought in by police. He has not drunk for 3 days. He is confused, agitated, has visual hallucinations (seeing insects), and a marked tremor. Pulse 120 bpm, BP 160/100 mmHg, temperature 38.2°C. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of confusion, autonomic hyperactivity, visual hallucinations, and tremor occurring 48-96 hours after last drink is pathognomonic of delirium tremens. Acute psychosis lacks autonomic features. Hepatic encephalopathy usually has asterixis and no hallucinations. Wernicke's presents with ataxia and ophthalmoplegia. Serotonin syndrome requires serotonergic drug exposure.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with known alcohol dependence presents after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. He last drank 24 hours ago. He has no history of epilepsy. Observations: pulse 110 bpm, BP 150/95 mmHg. Post-ictal, he is drowsy but orientated. Blood glucose normal. CT head normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Alcohol withdrawal seizures typically occur 12-48 hours after cessation. Normal CT and glucose make structural or metabolic causes less likely. No prior epilepsy history makes idiopathic epilepsy less likely. The temporal relationship to alcohol cessation is discriminating.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman, a daily drinker of 4 units of sherry, is admitted for hip fracture surgery. 24 hours post-op, she becomes acutely confused, agitated, and has a fine tremor. She is afebrile, but pulse 105 bpm. She has no known dementia. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Alcohol withdrawal is easily missed in elderly surgical patients. The timing (24 hours post-op, likely missing usual alcohol), autonomic signs (tachycardia, tremor), and absence of fever make infection less likely. Opioid withdrawal would have nausea, diarrhoea, and yawning. Hypoglycaemia would respond to dextrose. Postoperative cognitive decline is slower onset and lacks autonomic features.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol use presents with tremor, confusion, and sweating. He reports his last drink was 12 hours ago. Which investigation is most important to perform immediately to exclude a common medical mimic of alcohol withdrawal?
questions.explanation: Hypoglycemia can present identically to alcohol withdrawal. A finger-stick glucose is rapid, non-invasive, and can exclude this life-threatening mimic before attributing symptoms to withdrawal.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman attends a planned alcohol detoxification clinic. She has mild anxiety and a resting tremor but no hallucinations or autonomic instability. Which clinical tool is most appropriate to objectively assess the severity of her withdrawal?
questions.explanation: CIWA-Ar is a validated tool specifically for quantifying alcohol withdrawal severity. AUDIT, CAGE, and FAST are screening tools for alcohol misuse or dependence, not for acute withdrawal.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with alcohol dependence is admitted with delirium, tremor, and visual hallucinations. His temperature is 38.5°C. He has a history of head trauma one week ago. Which investigation is most appropriate to perform urgently?
questions.explanation: Given recent head trauma and altered mental status, CT head is essential to exclude intracranial pathology (e.g., subdural haematoma) before attributing delirium to alcohol withdrawal alone.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with alcohol withdrawal develops a new irregular pulse. An ECG shows atrial fibrillation. Which investigation is most appropriate to assess for a reversible cause of the arrhythmia?
questions.explanation: Electrolyte disturbances (e.g., hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia) are common in alcohol withdrawal and can provoke atrial fibrillation. Serum electrolytes are the first-line test to identify a correctable cause.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with a long history of alcohol dependence presents with progressive cognitive decline over six months. She has been abstinent for one year. Which blood test is most appropriate to identify a potentially reversible cause of her cognitive impairment?
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 deficiency is a reversible cause of dementia that is more common in chronic alcoholics due to poor diet or malabsorption. B12 levels should be checked before attributing cognitive decline to alcohol-related dementia.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a history of heavy daily alcohol use presents with tremor, sweating, and insomnia 12 hours after his last drink. Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol (CIWA-Ar) score is 12. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological management?
options: Chlordiazepoxide, 20 mg four times daily as needed
questions.explanation: For mild-to-moderate alcohol withdrawal (CIWA-Ar <15), first-line treatment is a symptom-triggered regimen with a long-acting benzodiazepine such as chlordiazepoxide. Diazepam IV is reserved for severe withdrawal. Haloperidol is not first-line and may lower seizure threshold. Carbamazepine is a second-line or not first-line. Acamprosate is for relapse prevention, not acute withdrawal.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with chronic alcohol dependence presents with confusion, agitation, visual hallucinations, and a heart rate of 120 bpm. He has no focal neurological signs, and his temperature is 37.8°C. CIWA-Ar score is 28. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Severe alcohol withdrawal with delirium tremens (CIWA-Ar >20, hallucinations, tachycardia) requires urgent treatment with a parenteral benzodiazepine; lorazepam IV is preferred due to reliable absorption. Oral diazepam or chlordiazepoxide are inappropriate in severe agitation. Haloperidol can be used adjunctively but is not first-line and may prolong QT interval. Phenobarbital is second-line or ICU use.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known alcohol dependence is admitted with a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. He has no history of epilepsy. His last drink was 24 hours ago. Examination reveals mild tremor but no confusion or hallucinations. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment to prevent further seizures?
questions.explanation: In alcohol withdrawal seizures, benzodiazepines are first-line to prevent recurrence. A long-acting benzodiazepine such as chlordiazepoxide is recommended for seizure prophylaxis and to treat withdrawal. Phenytoin and levetiracetam are not effective for alcohol withdrawal seizures unless there is an underlying epilepsy. Diazepam IV is for acute termination, but maintenance with a longer-acting agent is preferred.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation presents with severe alcohol withdrawal (CIWA-Ar 25). She is hemodynamically stable. Which agent is most appropriate as first-line treatment?
options: Chlordiazepoxide, 25 mg orally four times daily
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, benzodiazepines are used for alcohol withdrawal, but diazepam should be avoided due to accumulation in fetal tissues (long half-life, metabolite). Lorazepam is preferred as it has a shorter half-life and less placental accumulation. Chlordiazepoxide also has long half-life. Haloperidol and carbamazepine are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with a history of alcohol dependence completes a medically assisted withdrawal (detoxification). He is now abstinent from alcohol for 5 days. He is motivated to remain abstinent. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment for relapse prevention?
questions.explanation: For relapse prevention in alcohol dependence, NICE recommends acamprosate or naltrexone as first-line. Acamprosate is particularly effective in maintaining abstinence in recently detoxified patients. Naltrexone is also first-line but often used for reduction of heavy drinking. Disulfiram is second-line. Chlordiazepoxide is for acute withdrawal. Baclofen is not first-line (specialist use).

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known alcohol dependence presents with tremor, sweating, and anxiety for 24 hours. He drinks 12 units/day. Observations: HR 95, BP 135/85, afebrile. CIWA-Ar score is 8. No confusion or hallucinations. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer urgently to the alcohol liaison psychiatry team
Start oral chlordiazepoxide using a symptom-triggered regimen
Request a CT head to exclude intracranial pathology
Prescribe oral diazepam 10 mg four times daily
questions.explanation: This patient has mild-to-moderate alcohol withdrawal (CIWA-Ar <10). NICE guidelines recommend a symptom-triggered benzodiazepine regimen (e.g., chlordiazepoxide) as first-line treatment. Thiamine is important but not the immediate priority; it can be given alongside. Referral may be appropriate later, but the immediate step is to start withdrawal treatment to prevent progression. A fixed-dose diazepam regimen is not standard for mild withdrawal; chlordiazepoxide is preferred.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol use presents with confusion, agitation, visual hallucinations, and tremor. Observations: HR 120, BP 150/100, temperature 37.8°C. CIWA-Ar score is 28. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Request an emergency transfer to the intensive care unit
questions.explanation: This patient has severe alcohol withdrawal (delirium tremens). The immediate priority is to control agitation and prevent seizures with a benzodiazepine. IV lorazepam is preferred because of its rapid onset and predictable pharmacokinetics. Haloperidol may worsen QTc prolongation and is not first-line. Imaging is not the immediate step. Phenobarbital is not standard first-line. Transfer to ICU may be needed after initial stabilization, but the first action is medicating.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old homeless man with known alcohol dependence is brought to ED with confusion, ataxia, and bilateral horizontal nystagmus. He smells of alcohol. Observations are stable. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture to exclude meningitis
Give a bolus of intravenous 50% glucose
questions.explanation: This triad (confusion, ataxia, nystagmus) is classic for Wernicke's encephalopathy, a medical emergency. High-dose parenteral thiamine (Pabrinex) must be given immediately before any glucose, as glucose can precipitate or worsen the condition. Investigations (MRI, LP) should not delay treatment. Benzodiazepines are not indicated here as the primary issue is thiamine deficiency.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old pregnant woman (28 weeks gestation) with a history of alcohol dependence presents with mild tremor, anxiety, and nausea. She drinks 10 units/day. CIWA-Ar score is 9. She has no previous withdrawal seizures. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to maternity triage for observation without medication
Offer supportive care only and discharge
Start oral chlordiazepoxide using a symptom-triggered regimen
Start oral lorazepam using a symptom-triggered regimen
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, managing alcohol withdrawal is crucial to prevent harm to both mother and fetus. A benzodiazepine with a short half-life (e.g., lorazepam) is preferred to avoid accumulation and neonatal effects. Chlordiazepoxide has a long half-life and may increase fetal risk. Supportive care alone is insufficient for a CIWA-Ar of 9. Acamprosate is for maintenance, not withdrawal. Maternity triage involvement is warranted, but medication should not be delayed.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man has completed a 7-day alcohol detoxification program and has been abstinent for 2 weeks. He now wants medication to prevent relapse. His liver function tests show AST 85 U/L, ALT 70 U/L, and GGT 180 U/L. He has no contraindications. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start oral acamprosate 666 mg three times daily
Start oral naltrexone 50 mg once daily
Refer to local mutual aid groups (e.g., Alcoholics Anonymous)
Prescribe oral chlordiazepoxide as needed for cravings
Start oral disulfiram 200 mg once daily
questions.explanation: NICE recommends acamprosate or naltrexone for relapse prevention. Due to elevated LFTs, naltrexone is relatively contraindicated because of its hepatotoxicity. Acamprosate is safe in mild-to-moderate liver impairment. Disulfiram is second-line and also hepatotoxic. Mutual aid groups are beneficial but not pharmacological. Chlordiazepoxide is for withdrawal treatment, not relapse prevention.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man attends the Emergency Department with a two-day history of tremor, sweating, and anxiety after stopping his regular alcohol consumption. He has no previous history of withdrawal. Which single finding in his history or examination would mark him as high risk for severe withdrawal and warrant immediate escalation of care?
questions.explanation: A history of withdrawal seizures or delirium tremens is the strongest predictor of severe withdrawal; other findings are common but not specific for high risk.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman is on the ward for alcohol detoxification. She has mild tremor and anxiety, heart rate 100 bpm, blood pressure 130/85. She is receiving a fixed-dose chlordiazepoxide regimen. Over the past hour, she has become increasingly restless and confused. Which single clinical finding indicates that she is developing severe withdrawal and requires urgent medical review?
questions.explanation: In alcohol withdrawal, confusion or altered mental status is the hallmark of delirium tremens, a medical emergency. Other symptoms are features of mild/moderate withdrawal.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with chronic alcohol dependence presents with severe agitation, visual hallucinations, profuse sweating, and tremor. His temperature is 39.2°C, heart rate 140 bpm, blood pressure 160/90 mmHg. He is unable to follow commands. Which single clinical finding in this patient indicates the highest risk of mortality?
questions.explanation: Severe hyperthermia in the context of delirium tremens indicates massive autonomic hyperactivity and is a marker of life-threatening withdrawal; it requires urgent ICU-level care.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with known alcohol dependence presents to the Emergency Department with confusion, ataxia, and difficulty walking. He reports he stopped drinking three days ago and has been taking diazepam as prescribed. On examination, he has horizontal nystagmus on left gaze. Which neurological finding is a red flag for a specific complication rather than simple alcohol withdrawal?
questions.explanation: Nystagmus is a classic feature of Wernicke's encephalopathy, a neurological emergency due to thiamine deficiency. It is not part of uncomplicated alcohol withdrawal.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of alcohol-related cirrhosis is admitted for alcohol withdrawal. He has mild tremor and anxiety. On examination, he has a flapping tremor of his hands when asked to hold them out. Which single finding should prompt immediate concern for an alternative diagnosis that requires different management?
questions.explanation: Asterixis is a sign of hepatic encephalopathy, which can present similarly to alcohol withdrawal but is a distinct emergency requiring lactulose and reversal of precipitating factors, not simply benzodiazepines.
